segments.s533-g17: هرانکس که introduces a relative clause: “whoever / those who…”. بد زنده is a two‑word adjectival phrase where بد (ill/badly) modifies زنده (alive) to mean ‘badly alive’ or ‘wounded but alive’. زان رزمگاه is a prepositional phrase ‘from that battlefield’ (زان = from that, رزمگاه = battlefield). The whole segment functions as the noun phrase = “those who, badly alive, from that battlefield…”.
segments.s533-g18: سبک modifies the verb as an adverbial idea ‘light(ly) / without burden’ (i.e., empty‑handed); باز رفتند is a common compound meaning ‘returned / went back’ with باز = back/again and رفتند = they went. نزدِیک شاه gives the goal/presence ‘to/near the king’. Word order is adverb – verb – goal, with the subject omitted (understood from context).
segments.s533-g19: چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when’. آگاه شد is a predicative verb phrase ‘became aware’ (آگاه used as adjective/participial predicate plus شد ‘became’). The subject نامدار اردشیر (‘the renowned Ardashir’) follows the verb here (post‑verbal subject for emphasis), yielding ‘When the renowned Ardashir became aware…’.
segments.s533-g20: ازان introduces the content of his awareness: ‘of that / from that’. The coordinated nouns کشتن و غارت و دار و گیر are verbal‑nouns and nouns listed by و (‘and’): ‘killing and plunder and (taking of) dwellings/possessions and seizure’. Grammatically this is a noun phrase object of آگاه شد/‘learned of’.
segments.s533-g21: غمی گشت is an intransitive clause ‘a grief arose / it became grief’ with گشت = ‘became/turned into’. The coordinate clause لشکر همی باز خواند means ‘and (he) summoned the army again’: همی is an archaic adverbial particle (here ‘indeed/then’ or continuous marker), باز خواند = ‘called back / summoned’. The subject is omitted and understood from context.
segments.s533-g22: به زودی is an adverbial phrase ‘soon / immediately’. سلیح و درم are the direct objects ‘weapons and dirhams/coins’. برفشاند is the past tense verb ‘he scattered / cast about’, so the clause means ‘he quickly scattered weapons and money’. Word order places the adverbial before object and verb at the end (typical Persian).
segments.s533-g23: به تندی bi‑compound adverb ‘with haste / quickly’ (به + تندی). بیامد is ‘he came’ (past), and سوی هفتواد gives direction ‘toward Haftvād’. The verb precedes the directional phrase, with the subject again omitted (understood).
segments.s533-g24: به گردون برآمد is a poetic locution ‘rose to the sky/heavens’ (گردون = sky/fate, برآمد = rose/ascended). سر بدنژاد is the nominative phrase ‘the ignoble / low‑born head’ that follows the verb (post‑verbal subject). The whole line reads as a short inverted clause: ‘to the heavens rose the ignoble head’.
segments.s533-g49: This is a prepositional group introduced by از (from), listing three sources: آواز گوپال (the cry/voice of Gūpāl), وز ترگ (the gust/blast of the rag/streamer), و خود (and himself). Word order is typical Persian: the preposition از precedes the whole list, nouns follow, and the conjunction و links the final item. گوپال is a proper name here; آواز means “voice/cry.”
segments.s533-g50: همی is the old/poetic continuous marker meaning ‘kept/continually’; داد is the verb ‘gave’ (here transitive). گردون زمین را is the object phrase: گردون ‘the sky/heavens’ as subject giving درود ‘greeting’ to زمین را ‘the earth’ (را marks the definite direct object). درود is the noun ‘salutation/greeting’ receiving داد.
segments.s533-g51: تگ modifies بادپایان and functions as an attributive adjective before the noun phrase; بادپایان (‘fleet-footed’, ‘swift runners’) is a plural participial-type noun. زمین را is the definite object/locative sense governed by the verbal/adverbial کنان (a present-participle/adverbial form meaning ‘thus/so doing’ or ‘as they did’), indicating the manner in which they pressed/trampled the ground.
segments.s533-g52: در و دشت (doors and plains) is a coordinated noun phrase (در = gate/door, دشت = plain). شد is the past/resultive verb ‘became’; پر before a following noun means ‘full (of)’. سر بی‌تنان is a noun phrase ‘heads of the bodiless’ (بی‌تنان follows as a descriptive/attributive plural), so the clause says the gates and plains became full of those heads.
segments.s533-g53: برآن گونه is a frozen locution meaning ‘in that manner’ or ‘thus’; برآن (on/at that) + گونه (manner). شد is the past verb ‘became’. لشکر هفتواد is the subject noun phrase ‘the army of Haftvād’ (هفتواد is a proper name). Word order here is the topical adverbial phrase followed by the verb and the subject.
segments.s533-g54: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ‘that / as’, and گفتی here functions as ‘(as) you said’ (classical use of گفتی to report speech). بجنبید is a past/stative sense ‘moved/stirred’ (classical -id form marking the action), and دریا ز باد reads ‘the sea from/by the wind’ (ز = by/because of). Together the clause reports: ‘as you said, the sea was stirred by the wind.’
segments.s533-g55: بیابان is the subject ‘the desert/wilderness’. چنان شد is ‘became so’ (چنان = thus/so, شد = became). ز هر دو سپاه is an ablative phrase ‘from both hosts/armies’ (ز = from, هر دو = both, سپاه = host/army) indicating cause/agency for the change.
segments.s533-g56: که introduces the result clause ‘that…’. بر مور و بر پشه is a doubled prepositional frame ‘for/on the ant and for/on the gnat’ (بر = on/for, مور = ant, پشه = gnat/mosquito as a tiny insect). شد تنگ راه = ‘became (شد) narrow (تنگ) of way (راه)’ — idiomatically, ‘the way grew narrow even for ants and gnats’, i.e., the throng left no room.
segments.s533-g41: سپه (subject) + برکشید (past verb) is a standard S-V order here: 'the host raised/rose.' برکشید is past-tense, 3rd-person singular, and in epic Persian can mean 'to raise' or 'to take up (position)'. از + دو رویه دو صف marks source/arrangement: 'from two faces/sides, two rows' — the doubled 'دو ... دو ...' is poetic parallelism meaning two ranks on the two flanks.
segments.s533-g42: ز (poetic form of از) introduces the source for what follows; خورشید و شمشیر are coordinated nouns linked by و. برخاست is a past intransitive verb 'arose/rose up.' تف is an interjectional noun/onomatopoeia (a spitting or sharp explosive sound) that functions as the thing which 'arose' from sun and sword.
segments.s533-g43: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'; آواز کوس is a noun phrase 'the sound/cry of the war-drum' serving as the subject of آمد (past 'came'). از پشت پیل is a prepositional source phrase 'from behind the elephant,' with پشت = 'behind/rear.'
segments.s533-g44: همی is an emphatic/progressive particle often used in epic narrative to indicate immediate or vivid action; مرد بیهوش گشت is 'the man/hero became unconscious' (گشت = became). از marks cause ('because of'/'by'), so از دو میل indicates the instrument/cause (two masts/lances) that stunned him.
segments.s533-g45: برآمد (past 'arose/issued forth') comes first for stylistic emphasis, followed by the infinitive/participial noun خروشیدن 'roaring'; گاودم (lit. 'cow's tail') is the nominative/agent/source of that roar — a poetic image for a loud waving/tail or a tail-like standard that made noise.
segments.s533-g46: جهان پر شد is a predicative phrase 'the world became full'; شد is past 'became' and پر is the adjective 'full (of)'. از بانگ introduces the cause/instrument 'with the cry/sound of ...' and رویینه خم is an adjectival + noun unit: رویینه 'iron/armored' modifying خم 'curve/curved thing' (here a curved metal horn or similar instrument), the source of the cry.
segments.s533-g47: زمین is subject; جنب جنبان شد is a doubled verbal phrase (verb + intensive participial/adjacent form) meaning 'moved, quivered, and was set in motion' — an emphatic epic construction. از introduces cause: میخ نعل is a genitival compound 'the nail(s) of the horseshoe' (literally 'nail of the shoe'), i.e., the iron nails of hooves that made the ground tremble.
segments.s533-g48: هوا ('the air' or 'sky') is the subject; از درفش سران is an ablative/genitive phrase 'from the standards of the chiefs' (درفش = banner, سران = leaders/chiefs). گشت is past 'became/turned,' and لعل is the predicate nominal 'ruby' (i.e., ruby-red), so the line says the air turned ruby-red because of those banners.
segments.s533-g9: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. لشکر سراسر places the noun 'لشکر' (host/army) before the postpositive adjective 'سراسر' ('entire' or 'throughout'), and برآشوفتند is a past plural verb ('they rushed upon/they sprang up') placed at the end as is normal in Persian; the verb is an older/poetic form meaning they surged forward.
segments.s533-g10: به here functions instrumentally ('with' / 'by means of'), followed by the coordinated instruments گرز و تبرزین ('mace(s) and battle‑axe(es)'). همی is an older continuous/aspectual particle marking iterative or ongoing action, and کوفتند is the past plural verb 'they struck/beat' placed at the clause end.
segments.s533-g11: سپاه is the subject 'the host/army'. اندرآمد here functions as a past action 'entered/came out' (a poetic/archaic verbal form), and ز جای کمین is a prepositional phrase 'from the place of ambush' with ز = 'from' followed by جای 'place' and کمین 'ambush'. The clause follows S–V (subject then verb) with the source phrase after the verb.
segments.s533-g12: سیه شد is a two‑word predicate 'became black/dark' (سیه = black; شد = became). بران (= بر + ان) means 'upon/thereon/over that', attaching to نامداران زمین 'the renowned men of the land' (نامداران modified by زمین). The clause states a resultant state: the ground/scene was darkened over those famous men.
segments.s533-g13: کسی here functions in the negative sense 'no one' given the context (classical Persian often implies negation from context). بازنشناخت is a past verb meaning 'recognized again' or 'could (re‑)identify'; از introduces the measure/parts 'from' and پای دست is a coordinated noun phrase 'foot and hand' (here giving parts by which one might recognize someone). The whole phrase means that nobody could identify them even by feet or hands.
segments.s533-g14: تو گفتی is a reported‑speech head 'you said' (تو = 'you', گفتی = past 'you said' — note the provided glosses list speech‑nouns, but here it is the 2nd‑person past verb). زمین دست ایشان ببست is the reported clause: زمین ('the earth') as agent/scene, دست ایشان 'their hands' as object/possessive phrase, and ببست (ب + بست) is a past singular verb 'bound/tied' — i.e. 'you said that the earth had bound their hands.'
segments.s533-g15: ز کشته is a causal prepositional phrase 'because of/from the slain'. چنان شد is a copular phrase 'became thus/so'. در و دشت و کوه is a coordinated list 'dar (court/place), plain, and mountain' describing the scope/location affected; word order places the list after the copula.
segments.s533-g16: که introduces the result clause 'so that/that', پیروزگر is the subject 'the victor/conqueror', شد is 'became' (past), and ز + کشتن indicates cause/agency 'from killing' (i.e. through killing). ستوه is a predicate adjective 'weary/sick of'. Altogether: 'so that the conqueror became weary from killing.'
segments.s533-g25: بیاورد is a past third-person verb 'brought' (archaic/poetic order: verb before objects). The direct objects گنج و سلیح follow the verb; سلیح is an older form of سلاح 'weapon/arms'. از حصار is a simple prepositional phrase 'from the fortress' (از + noun).
segments.s533-g26: شد is the past copula 'became/was made'. خوار is a predicate adjective 'despised/humbled'; here the phrase (خوار شد) means 'was put to shame/was routed'. لشکر و کارزار is the grammatical subject placed after the verb for poetic effect: 'the army and the battle'. برو appears here not as an imperative but as an intensifying/poetic element attached to خوار (see explanation); its isolated modern senses don't capture this use.
segments.s533-g27: بود is the past copula 'was'. جدا (apart) and دور (distant) form the predication; ازو (poetic contraction of از + او) marks separation 'from him'. مهتر پسر is the noun phrase 'the chief's son' placed after the predicate (poetic inversion): literal order 'apart was from-him distant the chief son'.
segments.s533-g28: چو 'when' introduces a temporal clause. آگاه شد is a two-word predicate 'became aware/learned' (آگاه as adjectival/predicative element + شد 'became'). او is the subject ('he') placed after the predicate for emphasis. ز here is the poetic preposition 'of/about' and رزم پدر means 'his father's battle' ('رزم' = battle, modified by پدر).
segments.s533-g29: برآمد is the past 'arose/issued forth' and takes وز (a gust/blast) as its logical subject; ز آرام means 'from the calm'. خورد here is the impersonal/experiential verb 'befell' or 'fell (upon)' used with خواب as its subject/object — classical Persian idiom 'خواب خورد' = 'sleep overtook him'.
segments.s533-g30: بیامد is past 'came'. به کشتی is the goal phrase 'to/onto the ship'. برین is the fused phrase بر + این (= 'upon this') used adverbially; روی آب means 'on the surface of the water' with روی functioning as 'surface/on' and آب 'water'. Word order again shows poetic placement (goal and adverbial phrases follow the verb).
segments.s533-g31: را here marks the topical/definite phrase 'جهانجوی' (older Persian often uses را after the topic). نام ... بود is the copular clause 'the name was ...' so شاهوی functions as the predicate 'Shahoy' (a name in context even though شاهوی etymologically 'kingly'). Word order places the topic before the predicate 'name was Shahoy'.
segments.s533-g32: یکی 'a certain' (indefinite/topic marker) introduces the noun مرد 'man'. بدساز و بدگوی are coordinated descriptive epithets 'bad-maker' (troublemaker) and 'ill-speaker' (slanderer). بود is the past copula 'was', completing the description 'there was a man who was ...'.
segments.s533-g33: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' that marks origin; the phrase z + کشتی ('from the ship') precedes the verb بیامد ('came'), a simple past third‑person form. بر here is directional 'to/toward' and introduces the goal هفتواد (a proper name). The clause order is source — verb — goal, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s533-g34: دل هفتواد is a genitive construction 'the heart of Haftvād' (Haftvād's heart). از before پسر is causal 'because of / on account of the son'. گشت is the past of شدن 'to become' (here 'became'), and شاد is the predicate adjective 'happy/joyful' that follows the verb.
segments.s533-g35: بیاراست is a poetic past/perfective verb 'he arranged/arrayed'. بر میمنه is a locative phrase 'on the right (side)'; جای خویش = 'his place/station' with خویش realized as the possessive suffix ش. Word order places the verb before the locative phrase for emphasis.
segments.s533-g36: سپهبد is the title 'spahbed' (general). بد is used as an epithet ('bad'/'ill') attached to the title. و is the coordinating 'and'. لشکر آرای خویش is a noun phrase where آرای functions poetically as 'arrayed/ornamented' modifying لشکر ('army'), and خویش + ش marks possession 'his army.' The phrase thus presents the commander and his arrayed host.
segments.s533-g37: دو لشکر ('two armies') is the subject; بشد is the past of شدن used here as 'there became / were formed' (it happened that...). هر دو آراسته is a following adjectival phrase 'both arrayed' (هر دو = both, آراسته = adorned/arrayed). The verb marks the coming-into-being of the two hosts.
segments.s533-g38: پر از کینه is a prepositional phrase 'full of hatred' (پر 'full', از 'of', کینه 'hatred'). The next small clause is elliptical: سر گنج پر خواسته = 'their heads/minds were full (پر) of خواسته (desire) for گنج (treasure)'; خواسته functions as a noun 'wish/desire' and پر modifies it, yielding 'heads filled with desire for treasure.' Poetic word order places modifiers and causes compactly.
segments.s533-g39: بدیشان is a compound prepositional/adverbial 'toward them / at them'. نگه کرد is a light‑verb construction: نگه (gaze/look) + کرد (did) = 'he looked'. The subject شاه اردشیر follows the verb, a common poetic inversion placing action before agent; the tense is simple past.
segments.s533-g40: دل مرد is 'the man's/hero's heart' (دل + مرد). برنا شد is 'became youthful/vigorous' (برنا adjective + شد 'became'). از introduces the cause 'from/because of' and is followed by رنج پیر 'the old man's suffering' (رنج 'suffering', پیر 'old'). The clause therefore expresses that the man's heart grew vigorous as a result of the old man's hardship.
segments.s533-g1: چو 'when' introduces a temporal clause. آگه شد is the poetic/condensed form of آگاه شد 'became aware/learned' (شد = past 'became'). از marks the source or object of that awareness ('of/about Haftvād'), and اردشیر at the end is the subject (poetic word order puts the name after the verb).
segments.s533-g2: نبود is the past negative 'was not' (predicate). آن سخنها 'those words' is the grammatical subject, ورا is the older/dialectal form of 'to him', and دلپذیر is the predicate adjective 'pleasant/agreeable' placed after the object and indirect object — literally 'those words were not to him pleasant.'
segments.s533-g3: سپهبد is the subject 'the spahbed' and فرستاد is the simple past transitive verb 'sent/dispatched.' نزدیک اوی is an adverbial phrase 'near him / to his presence' (اوی = 'him' in poetic form), indicating destination or proximity of what was sent.
segments.s533-g4: سپاهی is a noun 'a troop/host' modified by the following epithets: بلند (here 'lofty' or 'great'), اختر (a poetic epithet 'star'/'bright one'), and رزمجوی 'war-seeking, combative.' The adjectives follow the noun, linked by و between اختر and رزمجوی, a normal epic/poetic ordering.
segments.s533-g5: چو 'when' introduces the clause. آگاه شد 'became aware/heard' uses the adjective آگاه with the past verb شد. زان سخن 'of that speech/that news' is the object introduced by زان ('from that'), and هفتواد at the end is the subject 'Haftvād' (again, name follows the verb in poetic order).
segments.s533-g6: ازیشان is the fused form of از + ایشان 'concerning/from them'; به دل در نیامدش is a compound predicate 'did not come into his heart' (در + آمد -> 'enter/came in', with -ش suffix referring back to the subject), and یاد is the noun 'memory/remembrance' that is the semantic subject of 'did not come.' The whole means 'no memory of them entered his heart.'
segments.s533-g7: کمینگاه کرد is a verb phrase 'set up/made an ambush' (کمینگاه = ambush, کردن = to make/set up). اندران is a locative 'therein/in that place' and کنج کوه 'the nook/corner of the mountain' specifies the location — together 'he set an ambush there in the mountain's corner.'
segments.s533-g8: بیامد is the past 'came.' سوی رزم خود is a directional phrase 'toward his battle' (خود here 'his own'/'his'), and با گروه 'with a troop/company' indicates accompaniment. Word order places the verb first, then directional and accompanying phrases.
segments.s533-g57: برین گونه is an older fused form of بر این گونه = “in this manner.” تا introduces a temporal limit “until.” روز برگشت is a noun phrase “the day of return” (برگشت here is a noun, ‘return’). زرد is a predicate/adjective that links forward to the verb in the next segment (i.e. “turned yellow”); in Persian poetry the adjective often appears before or separated from its verbal predicate.
segments.s533-g58: برآورد is the past tense of برآوردن (“brought forth/caused/raised”) and here supplies the verbal idea that connects with the preceding adjective زرد (“turned yellow”) — and then applies to the following image. شب is the logical agent “the night,” چادر is a metaphorical “tent,” and لاژورد is a poetic color noun “lapis/azure”; together شب چادر لاژورد = “the night (spread) a lapis‑lazuli tent.”
segments.s533-g59: ز هر سو is a poetic order for از هر سو = “from every side.” سپه is the army/host and functions as the object of خواند. باز خواند is a compound where باز (here “again/back”) modifies خواند (past of خواندن); خواند is used in the sense “to call, to summon,” so the phrase means “called (the) army back.” اردشیر is the agent and appears at the end (standard poetic word order: circumstantial phrase + object + verb + agent).
segments.s533-g60: پس starts this clause as a connective “then/thereupon.” پس پشت او is a locative phrase “behind him / at his rear.” بد is an adjective/adverb meaning “unfavourable/ill” modifying the following noun. یکی here is the indefinite sense “a certain / one,” and آبگیر is the noun “pond/reservoir.” The clause therefore describes that ‘behind him there was one unfavorable pond’ (inverted/condensed word order typical of poetry).
segments.s533-g61: چو (= چون) here means “when.” دریای is the genitive/construct form of دریا “the sea.” زنگارگون is an adjective ‘rust‑colored/tarnished,’ and شد is the past tense of شدن “became.” سیاه is a predicative adjective “black/dark” that complements شد. The whole clause reads as a temporal image: “when the sea became rust‑colored/black.”
segments.s533-g62: طلایه is the vanguard/forefront and is the grammatical subject of بیامد (past of آمدن, “came”). ز هر دو سپاه is a prepositional/agentive phrase “from both armies” (ز = from, هر دو = both, سپاه = armies); word order places the subject before the verb and the origin clause after it, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s533-g63: خورش literally means a cooked dish but here is used metonymically for ‘provisions/food’ belonging to the army. تنگ means “narrow/scant,” so خورش تنگ conveys that food had become scarce. بد functions adverbially/adjectivally as “badly/poorly” or “ill” modifying the situation for the army. لشکر شاه را is the noun phrase “the king’s army” with را marking it as the affected/definite group.
segments.s533-g64: که introduces an explanatory subordinate clause “because/for/that.” بدخواه is a noun “ill‑wisher/hostile man.” او is the possessive/agent “his” or “he” depending on context (here ‘his’/‘to him’). بسته is the past participle/adj. “closed/shut,” used transitively with راه = “road/way.” بد راه is a descriptive phrase “bad/unfavourable road,” and the final را is the object marker. The clause gives the reason: an enemy/ill‑wisher had shut off the road.

sentences.s533-s9: Those who came back alive, but badly wounded, from that battlefield returned to the king empty‑handed.
sentences.s533-s10: When the renowned Ardashir learned of that killing and plundering and of the seizure of goods.
sentences.s533-s11: Grief arose, and he summoned the army again; straightaway he scattered weapons and dirhams.
sentences.s533-s12: He hurried quickly toward Haftvād; the ignoble head rose to the heavens.
sentences.s533-s25: By the voice of Gūpāl, by the gust of the rag, and by himself, the heavens kept offering salutations to the earth.
sentences.s533-s26: With the fleet-footed runners trampling the ground, gates and plains became full of the heads of the bodiless.
sentences.s533-s27: Thus the army of Haftvād became, for as you said, the sea was stirred by the wind.
sentences.s533-s28: The wilderness so became from both hosts that, for ants and for gnats alike, the way grew narrow.
sentences.s533-s21: The host rose up in two ranks on both flanks; from the sun and the sword a spat (a sharp sound) arose.
sentences.s533-s22: When the sound of the war-drum came from behind the elephant, the warrior straightaway went senseless from the two masts/lances.
sentences.s533-s23: A roar rose from the ox-tail; the world was filled with the cry of the iron-curved (horns).
sentences.s533-s24: The earth quivered and shook from the nails of the horseshoes; the air turned ruby from the chiefs' standards.
sentences.s533-s5: When the whole host rushed forward, they struck with maces and battle‑axes.
sentences.s533-s6: The host came forth from its ambush‑place; the earth became dark over those famed men.
sentences.s533-s7: No one could recognize (them) by foot or hand; you said that the earth had bound their hands.
sentences.s533-s8: Because of the slain, town, plain, and mountain were such that the conqueror grew weary of killing.
sentences.s533-s13: He brought treasure and arms out of the fortress; the host and the fight were humbled.
sentences.s533-s14: The chief's son stood apart and distant from him; when he learned of his father's battle...
sentences.s533-s15: A gust rose from the calm and sleep fell upon him; he came to the ship onto this surface of the water.
sentences.s533-s16: The world-seeker's name was Shahoy; he was a certain man, a troublemaker and a slanderer.
sentences.s533-s17: Out of the ship he came to Haftvād; Haftvād's heart grew glad because of the boy.
sentences.s533-s18: He set his station on the right and arrayed the spahbed Bad and his army.
sentences.s533-s19: There became two armies, both arrayed, full of hatred and with their heads filled with desire for treasure.
sentences.s533-s20: King Ardashir looked at them; the man's heart became vigorous from the old man's suffering.
sentences.s533-s1: When Ardashir learned of Haftvād, those words were not pleasing to him.
sentences.s533-s2: The spahbed sent, to his vicinity, a host of lofty, star-like, war-seeking soldiers.
sentences.s533-s3: When Haftvād learned of that speech, no memory of them came into his heart.
sentences.s533-s4: He made an ambush in that nook of the mountain and, with his troop, came toward his battle.
sentences.s533-s29: Thus, until the returning day turned yellow, the night spread its lapis‑lazuli tent.
sentences.s533-s30: From every side Ardashir called the army back; behind him, however, lay a single ill‑favored pond.
sentences.s533-s31: When the sea grew rust‑colored and black, the vanguard of both armies came forward.
sentences.s533-s32: The king’s army’s provisions grew scarce, for a hostile man had closed off the (bad) road.
